title: Who Is Copying Who? Part III
text:
Who Is Copying Who? Part III

Louis-Vincent Gave

Gavekal Research | 21 Jul 2026

Back in the late 1990s, the argument for accepting China into the World Trade Organization was a simple one: if the Western world embraced trade with China, then, in time, China would become more “Western” and democratic. The logic behind this argument was compelling: Western values were superior, and delivered such obviously better outcomes, that anyone exposed to said values would end up embracing them with open arms. This argument had merit. After all, the West had just won the Cold War. Economies such as South Korea and Taiwan had thrived following their integration into global supply chains and, following years of surging economic growth, had eventually abandoned their ruthless military dictatorships for less arbitrary representative democratic systems. In short, the idea proposed in the late 1990s that, through trade, China would evolve to become more “like us” made a lot of sense. Of course, things did not quite work out that way…Quite the contrary! In a series of papers written during Covid (see Who Is Copying Who? and Who Is Copying Who? Part II), I argued that rather than China becoming more like the West, it often felt as if the West was becoming more like China. In Canada, pastors were arrested for celebrating mass. In the US, individuals were kicked off social media platforms for espousing “wrong think”. Which was better than the UK or Germany where individuals would start to receive visits from the police, and sometimes even face arrest and imprisonment for “wrong talk”. Vaccines were mandated across the population, regardless of preexisting immunity, religious belief or more simply, plain common sense. With Covid, the political cursor across the West moved from holding up “the rights of the individual” as the sacrosanct shining light of policymaking, to managing for the “optimal outcome for the community”. That was on the policy front. But interestingly, this “becoming more like China” is also becoming prevalent on the corporate side of things. Let me explain.
